title: League of Dungeoneers - The lowdown on the game flow
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kUDZK_9QPtA

Daniel (@dungeondive) gives the broad-overview tour of a League of Dungeoneers session — not a teaching video, but the closest he comes to one: the nuts and bolts of what actually happens between sitting down and packing up.

The flow: roll a starting settlement, run the First Blood skirmish to bloody your party, buy supplies, pick a quest, travel across the hex map (with a random travel event triggered on a D12 roll of 12 each day, themed to road, wilderness, or desert), arrive at the quest site, build the exploration deck from corridor and room cards plus the objective room and any side quests shuffled in. Then: explore. Each turn opens with a threat die that may trigger combat or non-combat threat events. Doors get rolled for locked/trapped status. Rooms have a 50% encounter chance, corridors 30%. Wandering monsters spawn at the entrance and creep inward, adding time pressure that rewards closing doors behind you.

Action economy walks through both one-point actions (move, attack, parry, search, quick spell) and two-point actions (power attack, charge, standard spell, search a whole room).

----

Wandering monsters as a time-pressure mechanic — yes please, or does it just turn dungeons into stressful sprints?
